---
title: "Enzyme- and temperature-constrained modelling of thermal performance curves in *Escherichia coli* (eciML1515): envelope, allocation and identifiability"
date: today
abstract: |
  We build an enzyme- and temperature-constrained genome-scale model (etc-GEM) of
  *Escherichia coli* — the GECKO formulation eciML1515 of the manually curated
  iML1515 reconstruction — in which every enzyme's turnover follows a
  macromolecular-rate-theory (MMRT) temperature response and total enzyme demand is
  bounded by a shared proteome pool, so that growth thermal performance curves (TPCs)
  emerge from the interaction of a thermal catalytic *envelope* and proteome
  *allocation*. Following Li et al. and the MRes of Madkaikar, the high-temperature
  collapse is set by a **data-grounded two-state unfolding model**: each enzyme's
  native fraction is keyed on its melting temperature $T_m$ (from the *E. coli* melting
  proteome and sequence-based optima; grounded for 90 % of enzymes), so the falling
  limb and $CT_\text{max}$ arise from denaturation, mechanistically decoupled from the
  rising-limb activation energy. The nominal model reproduces a realistic *E. coli*
  curve ($T_\text{opt} = 40\,°\text{C}$, $r_\text{max} = 0.56\,\text{h}^{-1}$,
  $E_a = 0.72\,\text{eV}$, and a now-realistic $CT_\text{max} = 47\,°\text{C}$ set by
  $T_m$), fitting empirical *E. coli* growth data with $R^2 \approx 0.86$. A global
  sensitivity sweep and a Shapley variance decomposition reveal that, once the envelope
  is grounded per enzyme, **the thermal envelope dominates the variance of every TPC
  descriptor — including the rate** ($r_\text{max}$ is 78 % envelope, not the ~100 %
  allocation seen under the older peak-normalised convention); allocation contributes a
  secondary ~20 % to the rate and optimum. A proteome-wide per-enzyme control analysis
  finds thermal control concentrated in very few enzymes (led by
  argininosuccinate synthase) and only ~2 % of per-enzyme parameters
  identifiable from growth alone, motivating omics constraints; correlated,
  DLTKcat-informed sampling sharpens the physically pinned descriptors (e.g.
  $CT_\text{max}$ IQR 0.2 vs 1.2 °C); and a coarse-grained sector model yields an
  interior growth optimum at intermediate metabolic allocation. The apparent
  "allocation sets magnitude, envelope sets shape" division of labour is thus shown to
  be **largely a structural artifact of peak-normalisation**; distinguishing a
  genome-set envelope from an allocation-set magnitude is not resolvable from growth
  data alone and will require temperature-resolved proteome and flux calibration. All
  results are structural in-silico predictions pending phenotype/omics calibration.
---

# Background and objectives

The broader aim of this work is to **predict microbial thermal performance curves
(TPCs) from genomes**. The temperature dependence of microbial growth, respiration
and carbon-use efficiency governs how microbial communities respond to warming, and
therefore how much of the terrestrial carbon store is stabilised or lost as the
climate changes. Scaling that response from cells to communities and to
carbon-cycle feedbacks requires a way to predict a taxon's TPC from information that
scales — ideally its genome. Because a cell's TPC is an emergent property of its
metabolism operating under a finite, temperature-sensitive enzyme budget, an
enzyme- and temperature-constrained genome-scale metabolic model (etc-GEM) is a
natural mechanistic predictor: it turns a genome and a set of enzyme kinetic
parameters into a growth rate at any temperature.

We establish and de-risk the method on **_Escherichia coli_**, using the
enzyme-constrained model **eciML1515** — a GECKO formulation [@Sanchez2017;
@Domenzain2022] of the manually curated iML1515 reconstruction [@Monk2017]. *E.
coli* / iML1515 is the best-curated bacterial genome-scale model, with the
best-characterised turnover numbers ($k_\text{cat}$), so it is the right place to
build, test and stress the modelling machinery before scaling it to a large,
phylogenetically diverse bacterial isolate library where curation and kinetic data
are far sparser.

The in-silico work is organised around six objectives, which the rest of the report
returns to:

- **O1** — reproduce a realistic *E. coli* growth TPC and define its components.
- **O2** — determine which model components set the thermal **envelope**
  ($T_\text{opt}$, $CT_\text{max}$, breadth, $E_a$), and whether a few enzymes
  dominate (the basis for sequence-predictability).
- **O3** — determine what sets the rate **magnitude** / baseline rate ($r_\text{max}$),
  i.e. proteome allocation.
- **O4** — quantify the **separability** of a genome-set envelope from an
  allocation-set magnitude (the key hypothesis).
- **O5** — assess **identifiability**: which parameters are inferable from growth data
  alone versus which require omics.
- **O6** — move from a nominal parameter scan to **calibrated uncertainty** using
  sequence/temperature-aware $k_\text{cat}$ predictions.

# The model: an enzyme- and temperature-constrained genome-scale model

The etc-GEM links four layers; understanding how they connect is what motivates the
experimental design.

**Metabolic network.** The stoichiometric backbone and gene–protein–reaction (GPR)
rules are those of iML1515, a genome-scale reconstruction of *E. coli* K-12 MG1655
covering 1515 genes [@Monk2017]. Flux balance analysis on this network is solved
with cobrapy [@Ebrahim2013].

**Enzyme constraints (GECKO).** Each reaction is coupled to the enzyme(s) that
catalyse it: carrying flux $v_i$ costs enzyme mass $v_i / k_{\text{cat},i}$, and the
summed cost of all enzymes is bounded by a shared **protein pool**. This is the
GECKO enzyme-constraint formulation [@Sanchez2017; @Domenzain2022]; the eciML1515
model is taken from the SysBioChalmers ecModels container
(<https://github.com/SysBioChalmers/ecModels>). The pool makes proteome allocation a
first-class, limiting resource: at the growth optimum the pool binds, so faster flux
through one pathway must come at the expense of another.

**Temperature layer.** Each enzyme's turnover number is made temperature-dependent
through macromolecular rate theory (MMRT), in which a change in the heat capacity of
activation, $\Delta C_p^\ddagger$, curves the Arrhenius plot so that $k_\text{cat}(T)$
rises to an optimum $T_\text{opt}$ and sets the rising-limb apparent activation energy
$E_a$ [@Hobbs2013]. The etc-GEM supports two thermal modes for how the
high-temperature *collapse* of usable activity arises.

*Two-state unfolding mode (default).* Following Li et al. [@Li2021] and the MRes of
Madkaikar [@Madkaikar2023], the effective per-flux enzyme cost is
$\text{cost}_i(T) = \text{base}_i / [\,\text{rel-}k_{\text{cat},i}(T)\cdot f_{N,i}(T)\,]$,
where $\text{rel-}k_{\text{cat},i}(T)$ is the MMRT turnover factor anchored at
$T_\text{opt}$ and $f_{N,i}(T)\in[0,1]$ is the **native (folded) fraction** from a
two-state native$\leftrightarrow$unfolded equilibrium keyed on the per-enzyme melting
temperature $T_m$. As $T\to T_m$, $f_N\to 0$, enzyme cost diverges and growth
collapses, so the **falling limb and $CT_\text{max}$ are set by denaturation ($T_m$),
mechanistically decoupled from the rising-limb $E_a$** (set by $k_\text{cat}(T)$ and
$T_\text{opt}$). This is the data-grounded model: per-enzyme $T_m$ comes from the
*E. coli* melting proteome [@Leuenberger2017] and per-enzyme $T_\text{opt}$ from
sequence-based catalytic-optimum predictions [@LiEngqvist2019] (assembled by the MRes
for iML1515-family models; coverage for eciML1515 is reported below). This mode also
adds a temperature-dependent non-growth-associated maintenance (NGAM) term that rises
with $T$ above a basal floor, as in the reference model. For other library strains
without measured values, sequence predictors (DeepET/TOMER-type for $T_\text{opt}$/$T_m$)
are the route to per-enzyme parameters; they are not needed for *E. coli* here.

*Peak-normalised MMRT mode (alternative).* Here each enzyme's reference $k_\text{cat}$
is treated as its own maximum (peak-normalisation), so any temperature deviation only
raises cost and the MMRT curvature alone sets both breadth and $E_a$; the curvature
$\Delta C_p^\ddagger$ can be calibrated by bisection so the nominal $E_a$ matches a
target (e.g. 0.65 eV, the conserved metabolic-theory value [@Davidi2016]). This mode
couples breadth to $E_a$ and lets the fitted curvature push $CT_\text{max}$ to
unrealistically high values; it is retained as a backward-compatible baseline, and the
unfolding mode is preferred precisely because it grounds the collapse in $T_m$.

**Proteome allocation and sectors.** In the baseline model the shared pool is a
single scalar. Following the bacterial growth laws [@Basan2015; @Scott2010], it can
be refined into three coarse-grained **sectors** — metabolic enzymes ($f_\text{metab}$),
biosynthesis/ribosomes ($f_\text{bio}$, a translation cap on growth) and
maintenance/housekeeping ($f_\text{maint}$) — that sum to one. This makes the
baseline rate an explicit allocation trade-off rather than a single number.

**$k_\text{cat}(T)$ data and the in-vitro/in-vivo gap.** Temperature-dependent
turnover numbers are drawn from DLTKcat, a deep-learning predictor of
sequence- and temperature-aware $k_\text{cat}$ [@Qiu2024]
(<https://github.com/SizheQiu/DLTKcat>), fitted per enzyme to MMRT parameters
($T_\text{opt}$, $\Delta C_p^\ddagger$). Data-driven $k_\text{cat}$ are needed
because in-vitro turnover numbers systematically mispredict in-vivo apparent rates
[@Davidi2016; @Heckmann2020]. This etc-GEM construction builds directly on the
Bayesian temperature-constrained GEM programme [@Li2021; @Pettersen2023].

**How the linked structure maps onto the design.** Because the organismal TPC emerges
from a thermal **envelope** (per-enzyme $k_\text{cat}(T)$) acting through a binding
proteome **allocation** budget, the model exposes two natural, largely separable
axes to perturb: thermal-envelope parameters and proteome allocation. The
sensitivity, decomposition and control experiments below interrogate exactly these
two axes to address O2–O5.

# In-silico experimental design and key assumptions

We run five complementary analyses, each targeting specific objectives:

- **Global sensitivity** (O2, O3): a Latin-hypercube sweep over envelope knobs
  (a uniform shift $dT_\text{opt}$ of all enzyme optima, a spread factor
  `topt_scale`, and a curvature scaling `dCp_scale`) and an allocation knob
  (`budget_scale`), reducing each sampled TPC to descriptors and computing Spearman
  rank sensitivities.
- **Allocation-vs-envelope variance decomposition** (O4): a crossed two-group design
  (allocation samples × envelope samples), with each descriptor's variance split into
  allocation, envelope and interaction components via an exact two-group Shapley
  attribution. The allocation axis here is the mechanistic proteome-sector split
  ($f_\text{metab}$, $f_\text{maint}$), not a scalar budget.
- **Per-enzyme thermal control and identifiability** (O2, O5): a cheap proteome-wide
  screen (usage share × analytic thermal sensitivity) over all enzymes, refined by
  targeted finite-difference control coefficients on the highest-control (top-K)
  enzymes; identifiability is reported as a first-order control-magnitude proxy.
- **DLTKcat-calibrated envelope and calibrated sampling** (O6): a sweep whose nominal
  envelope is the DLTKcat per-enzyme fit, and a calibrated ensemble that samples the
  envelope per enzyme with a one-factor correlation (shared fraction $\rho$), with an
  option to draw per-enzyme means and standard deviations from the DLTKcat posterior.
- **Proteome-sector allocation trade-off** (O3): a sweep over the sector split
  ($f_\text{metab}$, $f_\text{maint}$) to expose the interior growth optimum.

**Key assumptions**, stated plainly so results can be read appropriately:

1. **Two-state unfolding thermal model (default).** The high-$T$ collapse is set by
   per-enzyme denaturation ($T_m$), which is held at its grounded value rather than
   swept; the reference $k_\text{cat}$ is anchored at $T_\text{opt}$. (The alternative
   peak-normalised MMRT mode instead treats $k_{\text{cat,ref}}$ as each enzyme's
   maximum, coupling breadth to $E_a$.)
2. The proteome **pool operates in the binding regime**, so allocation is limiting at
   the optimum.
3. **Whole-cell $CT_\text{max}$ tracks the enzyme melting temperatures**: growth
   requires the whole proteome folded, so the organism collapses below the median
   enzyme $T_m$; $T_m$ values are grounded (melting proteome / sequence predictors),
   not fitted to the growth curve.
4. The **one-factor $\rho$** used in calibrated sampling is a simple stand-in for the
   true (unknown) covariance of thermostability across enzymes.
5. **Variance fractions are defined relative to the swept input ranges** — they
   describe what the model *can* generate under those ranges, not a claim about real
   populations.
6. **Identifiability is first-order** (a control-magnitude proxy), not a full
   Fisher-information / profile-likelihood analysis.
7. **DLTKcat skill is limited** (log-scale $R^2 \approx 0.6$ [@Qiu2024]), and only a
   subset of enzymes currently have usable fits.
8. Only **growth** is modelled here; respiration and carbon-use efficiency — the
   community-relevant currencies — are future work.
9. All results are **structural / in-silico predictions** pending phenotype and omics
   calibration.

# Results

## Nominal TPC on eciML1515 (O1)

The nominal (unperturbed) TPC and its sampled ensemble are shown in @fig-tpc; the
descriptors are in @tbl-nominal. In the data-grounded two-state unfolding model
(grounded per-enzyme $T_m$/$T_\text{opt}$ for 2308/2560 = 90 % of enzymes, the rest at
dataset means), the model reproduces a realistic mesophilic *E. coli* growth curve:
**optimum $T_\text{opt} = 40\,°\text{C}$** and **maximum rate
$r_\text{max} = 0.56\,\text{h}^{-1}$** (a ~1.2 h doubling time, in the right range for
aerobic growth on a defined medium), built from 2560 enzyme constraints under a pool
budget of 0.082, with a rising-limb **$E_a = 0.72\,\text{eV}$** (69 kJ mol⁻¹). Now that
the high-temperature collapse is set by denaturation rather than by a fitted MMRT
curvature, the critical temperature **$CT_\text{max} = 46.8\,°\text{C}$** and breadth
($37.8\,°\text{C}$) are biologically realistic for *E. coli* — the falling limb is a
sharp denaturation collapse near the enzyme melting temperatures (median $T_m
\approx 56\,°\text{C}$; whole-cell $CT_\text{max}$ lower because growth needs the
*whole* proteome folded and functional). Against the MRes empirical *E. coli* growth
data the nominal curve reproduces the TPC shape with $R^2 \approx 0.86$ (cf. 0.94 for
the MRes model on its own iJO1366 reconstruction). The cold critical point sits near
the grid floor ($CT_\text{min} \approx 9\,°\text{C}$), so $CT_\text{min}$ and the
niche width remain lower bounds set partly by the temperature grid. Objective O1 is
met — and, relative to the peak-normalised MMRT baseline (which gave an unrealistic
$CT_\text{max} \approx 76\,°\text{C}$), the unfolding model also fixes the upper
thermal limit.

![**Nominal TPC ensemble for eciML1515.** Grey lines are individual sampled curves across proteome allocation and $k_\text{cat}(T)$ perturbations; the blue band is the interquartile range and the solid line the ensemble median, with the nominal curve dashed.](assets/figures/tpc_ensemble.png){#fig-tpc fig-pos="H" width=80%}

```{python}
#| label: tbl-nominal
#| tbl-cap: "Nominal TPC descriptors for eciML1515 read from the sweep summary (optimum temperature, maximum growth rate, critical temperatures, thermal breadth and apparent activation energy)."
#| output: asis
import json
import pandas as pd

with open("assets/tables/summary.json") as fh:
    nom = json.load(fh)["nominal"]
rows = [
    ("T_opt (°C)",         nom["Topt_C"]),
    ("r_max (1/h)",        nom["rmax"]),
    ("CT_min (°C)",        nom["CTmin_C"]),
    ("CT_max (°C)",        nom["CTmax_C"]),
    ("Niche width (°C)",   nom["niche_width_C"]),
    ("E_a (eV)",           nom["Ea_eV"]),
]
df = pd.DataFrame(rows, columns=["Descriptor", "Nominal"])
df["Nominal"] = df["Nominal"].round(3)
print(df.to_markdown(index=False))
```

## Global sensitivity: what generates TPC variation (O2, O3)

A Latin-hypercube sweep over the envelope knobs ($dT_\text{opt}$, `topt_scale`,
`dCp_scale`) and allocation (`budget_scale`) yields the descriptor distributions of
@fig-descdist and the Spearman sensitivity matrix of @fig-sens and @tbl-sens. Because
the denaturation temperatures $T_m$ are held at their grounded values (not swept), the
sensitivity pattern differs instructively from the peak-normalised baseline:

- **Position ($T_\text{opt}$) tracks the envelope shift.** $T_\text{opt}$ correlates
  strongly with the uniform optimum shift $dT_\text{opt}$ ($\rho = 0.83$) and the
  spread (`topt_scale`, $\rho = 0.54$); allocation has essentially no effect
  ($\rho = -0.08$). This is the envelope setting curve position — the basis of
  sequence-predictability (O2).
- **Magnitude ($r_\text{max}$) is allocation-set but envelope-coupled.**
  $r_\text{max}$ rises with the pool budget (`budget_scale`, $\rho = 0.58$; O3) but
  is also *depressed* by envelope shifts ($dT_\text{opt}$ $\rho = -0.53$,
  `topt_scale` $\rho = -0.34$): pushing enzyme optima up toward the fixed $T_m$
  compresses the usable window between $T_\text{opt}$ and the denaturation collapse,
  lowering peak rate. Unlike the peak-normalised model — where $r_\text{max}$ was a
  pure function of the budget — grounding $T_m$ couples magnitude to the envelope.
- **The upper limit and breadth track the optimum shift, not the curvature.** With
  $T_m$ fixed, $CT_\text{max}$ is governed by $dT_\text{opt}$ ($\rho = 0.84$) rather
  than by `dCp_scale` ($\rho = -0.06$), and niche width falls with $dT_\text{opt}$
  ($\rho = -0.63$) as the peak approaches the (fixed) denaturation point. The
  rising-limb $E_a$ is still set by the MMRT curvature (`dCp_scale` $\rho = 0.72$).

So the tidy one-input-per-descriptor picture of the peak-normalised model gives way to
a more coupled — and more physical — pattern once the collapse is pinned to $T_m$: the
thermal limits move with the optimum through a fixed denaturation ceiling, and only
$E_a$ remains a clean function of the curvature.

![**Global sensitivity of TPC descriptors.** Spearman rank-correlation between each swept input (rows) and each TPC descriptor (columns); red is positive, blue negative.](assets/figures/sensitivity_heatmap.png){#fig-sens fig-pos="H" width=90%}

![**Descriptor distributions across the sweep.** Marginal distribution of each TPC descriptor over the Latin-hypercube ensemble.](assets/figures/descriptor_distributions.png){#fig-descdist fig-pos="H" width=90%}

```{python}
#| label: tbl-sens
#| tbl-cap: "Spearman sensitivity indices (input × TPC descriptor) for the headline descriptors from the global sweep."
#| output: asis
import pandas as pd

s = pd.read_csv("assets/tables/sensitivity_spearman.csv", index_col=0)
keep = [c for c in ["Topt_C", "rmax", "CTmax_C", "niche_width_C", "Ea_eV"] if c in s.columns]
print(s[keep].round(2).to_markdown())
```

## Allocation vs envelope decomposition (O4)

To move beyond one-at-a-time sensitivities we partition each descriptor's *variance*
over a crossed allocation × envelope design into allocation, envelope and interaction
components (Shapley-normalised fractions $\varphi_A$, $\varphi_E$; @tbl-decomp,
@fig-decompvar, @fig-decompshap). Crucially, the allocation axis here is the
**mechanistic proteome-sector split** ($f_\text{metab}$, $f_\text{maint}$;
[@Basan2015; @Scott2010]), not a single scalar budget — this matters because a
scalar budget can only rescale the pool and *by construction* cannot move
$T_\text{opt}$ or $CT_\text{max}$, which would inflate the apparent separability.
@fig-decompach shows the descriptor ranges reachable by allocation alone versus
envelope alone.

In the grounded unfolding model the decomposition tells a **strikingly different — and
more honest — story than the peak-normalised baseline**: the thermal **envelope
dominates the variance of every descriptor**, including the rate.

- **The thermal limits and shape are almost pure envelope**: $CT_\text{max}$,
  $CT_\text{min}$ and niche width have $\varphi_E = 0.997,\ 0.997,\ 0.996$, with
  $E_a$ at $\varphi_E = 0.97$ and skewness at $0.90$.
- **$T_\text{opt}$ is envelope-dominated** ($\varphi_E = 0.79$, $\varphi_A = 0.21$,
  interaction $S_{AE} = 0.16$), as is the 80 %-breadth descriptor $B_{80}$
  ($\varphi_E = 0.90$).
- **Most importantly, $r_\text{max}$ is now envelope-dominated too**
  ($\varphi_E = 0.78$, $\varphi_A = 0.22$) — not the ~100 % allocation of the
  peak-normalised model.

The clean "allocation sets magnitude, envelope sets shape" separation therefore **does
not survive** once the envelope is grounded per enzyme. The reason is mechanistic and
was hidden by the earlier convention: with peak-normalised $k_\text{cat}$ the enzyme
cost at the optimum is independent of the envelope, so $r_\text{max}$ could only track
the pool budget; once the melting temperatures are fixed and the reference $k_\text{cat}$
is anchored at $T_\text{opt}$, shifting the optima toward the (fixed) denaturation
ceiling depresses the peak rate, so the envelope drives $r_\text{max}$ as well. This is
the honest, mechanistic reading of O4: **the apparent genome-set-envelope / allocation-
set-magnitude split was largely a structural artifact of peak-normalisation; under the
data-grounded model the two axes are not separable, and the envelope is the dominant
control on the whole TPC** — with allocation contributing a real but secondary
~20 % to the rate and optimum. The informative quantities going forward are these
allocation shares and the interaction terms, measured across genomes, rather than a
claim of fixed separability (see Interpretation).

![**Achievable descriptor ranges.** Ranges of each TPC descriptor reachable by varying allocation alone versus the thermal envelope alone.](assets/figures/decomp_achievable.png){#fig-decompach fig-pos="H" width=85%}

![**Variance partition.** Variance of each descriptor attributed to allocation, envelope and their interaction.](assets/figures/decomp_variance.png){#fig-decompvar fig-pos="H" width=85%}

![**Shapley effects.** Shapley-normalised allocation and envelope contributions to descriptor variance.](assets/figures/decomp_shapley.png){#fig-decompshap fig-pos="H" width=85%}

```{python}
#| label: tbl-decomp
#| tbl-cap: "Variance of each TPC descriptor partitioned into allocation (A), envelope (E) and interaction (AE) components, with Shapley-normalised allocation/envelope fractions (φ_A, φ_E), from the H1.3 decomposition on the mechanistic sector allocation axis."
#| output: asis
import pandas as pd

d = pd.read_csv("assets/tables/decomposition_table.csv")
cols = [c for c in ["descriptor", "V_A", "V_E", "V_AE", "phi_A", "phi_E"] if c in d.columns]
print(d[cols].round(3).to_markdown(index=False))
```

## Per-enzyme thermal control and identifiability (O2, O5)

We next ask *which* enzymes set the envelope, and *which* per-enzyme parameters could
be recovered from growth data alone. @fig-ctrlthermal and @tbl-control rank enzymes
by a thermal-screen control score; @fig-ctrlbottle shows how the rate-limiting enzyme
shifts with temperature; @fig-ctrlident and @tbl-ident summarise identifiability.

**Thermal control is concentrated in very few enzymes (O2).** The top thermal
determinant is **argininosuccinate synthase** (`ARGSSNo1`, enzyme `P0A6E4`), with a
control coefficient $\mathrm{CC}[CT_\text{max}, T_{\text{opt},i}] = 0.015$; the screen
score then falls essentially to zero for the next enzyme. (The control of the *optimum*
$T_\text{opt}$ on any single enzyme is ≈ 0 because $T_\text{opt}$ is an argmax
descriptor — a discrete jump — so single-enzyme envelope control instead registers on
the interpolated $CT_\text{max}$; in the unfolding model the per-enzyme control is
computed on $T_\text{opt}$, while the falling limb is governed by the fixed $T_m$
values.) A handful of enzymes therefore dominate the organism's thermal envelope,
which is precisely the property that makes the envelope potentially predictable from
sequence (O2).

**Most per-enzyme parameters are not identifiable from growth alone (O5).**
Computed **proteome-wide** — over all 2560 enzymes × three parameters ($T_{\text{opt},i}$,
$\Delta C_{p,i}$, $k_{\text{cat},i}$) = 7680 parameters — only **172 (2.2 %)** clear the
identifiability threshold, and essentially all of them lie within the 300
finite-difference-refined high-control enzymes (**172/300 = 57 %** among that
top-K subset; @tbl-ident). Reporting only the top-K subset would therefore overstate
identifiability more than twenty-fold; the proteome-wide view shows that growth alone
constrains only a small, high-control subset and that the remaining ~98 % of parameters
require proteome/flux (omics) data to pin down — the central motivation for the omics
arm of the programme (O5).

![**Per-enzyme thermal control.** Top enzymes ranked by their thermal-screen control score over the TPC.](assets/figures/control_thermal.png){#fig-ctrlthermal fig-pos="H" width=85%}

![**Bottleneck enzyme vs temperature.** Identity/strength of the rate-limiting enzyme as a function of temperature.](assets/figures/control_bottleneck.png){#fig-ctrlbottle fig-pos="H" width=85%}

![**Identifiability of per-enzyme thermal parameters.** Distribution of the first-order identifiability score across enzyme × parameter combinations, with the identifiability threshold marked.](assets/figures/control_identifiability.png){#fig-ctrlident fig-pos="H" width=75%}

```{python}
#| label: tbl-control
#| tbl-cap: "Top enzymes by thermal-screen control score, with their reaction and enzyme identifiers."
#| output: asis
import pandas as pd

c = pd.read_csv("assets/tables/thermal_control.csv")
cols = [x for x in ["rank", "rxn_id", "enzyme_id", "thermal_screen"] if x in c.columns]
print(c[cols].head(10).round(4).to_markdown(index=False))
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-ident
#| tbl-cap: "Identifiability of per-enzyme thermal/catalytic parameters from the growth TPC, computed proteome-wide (all enzymes × {Topt_i, dCp_i, kcat_i}) as a first-order control-magnitude proxy. The proteome-wide identifiable fraction is small, whereas among the top-K finite-difference control enzymes (`refined`) a much larger fraction is identifiable — few enzymes dominate control (O2) while most parameters need omics constraints (O5)."
#| output: asis
import pandas as pd

idf = pd.read_csv("assets/tables/identifiability.csv")
n = len(idf)
n_ident = int(idf["identifiable_from_growth"].sum())
ref = idf[idf["refined"]] if "refined" in idf.columns else idf.iloc[0:0]
n_ref = len(ref)
n_ident_ref = int(ref["identifiable_from_growth"].sum()) if n_ref else 0
frac = f"{n_ident}/{n} ({n_ident/n:.1%})" if n else "n/a"
frac_topk = f"{n_ident_ref}/{n_ref} ({n_ident_ref/n_ref:.1%})" if n_ref else "n/a"
summ = pd.DataFrame({
    "quantity": ["parameters (enzymes × 3)",
                 "identifiable from growth (proteome-wide)",
                 "identifiable among top-K control enzymes",
                 "mean identifiability score"],
    "value": [n, frac, frac_topk, round(idf["ident"].mean(), 3)],
})
print(summ.to_markdown(index=False))
```

## DLTKcat-calibrated envelope (O6)

Overlaying per-enzyme $T_\text{opt}$ fitted from DLTKcat $k_\text{cat}(T)$ predictions
[@Qiu2024] onto the grounded unfolding envelope gives the ensemble in @fig-dltkcat —
a second, orthogonal data-driven source for the rising-limb parameters (O6). In the
current fit, usable DLTKcat parameters were available for only **13 of 2560 enzymes**,
and those enzymes are not pool-limiting at the optimum, so the DLTKcat-overlaid nominal
TPC coincides with the grounded default ($T_\text{opt} = 40\,°\text{C}$,
$r_\text{max} = 0.56\,\text{h}^{-1}$, $CT_\text{max} = 46.8\,°\text{C}$,
$E_a = 0.72\,\text{eV}$). The run therefore demonstrates that the DLTKcat → MMRT →
etc-GEM pipeline is wired end-to-end, but a materially different data-driven envelope
will require DLTKcat fits for a much larger, and rate-limiting, set of enzymes.

![**DLTKcat-calibrated TPC ensemble.** TPC ensemble using per-enzyme MMRT parameters fitted from DLTKcat temperature-dependent $k_\text{cat}$ predictions.](assets/figures/dltkcat_ensemble.png){#fig-dltkcat fig-pos="H" width=80%}

## Calibrated thermal-envelope uncertainty (O6)

Beyond a single data-driven point, we move from a nominal parameter scan to
**calibrated uncertainty** by sampling the thermal envelope *per enzyme* with a
one-factor correlation structure (shared fraction $\rho = 0.7$; per-enzyme
$T_\text{opt}$ sd 4 K, $\Delta C_p$ sd 30 %), with an option to draw per-enzyme means
and standard deviations from the DLTKcat posterior (`mode: posterior`). @fig-calens
shows the calibrated-uncertainty fan; @fig-calvsdef overlays the default
hand-set-range spread against the calibrated spread; @tbl-calibrated gives the
calibrated medians and IQRs.

The calibrated ensemble is centred on the nominal descriptors (median $T_\text{opt} =
41\,°\text{C}$, $r_\text{max} = 0.53\,\text{h}^{-1}$, $CT_\text{max} = 46.8\,°\text{C}$,
$E_a = 0.75\,\text{eV}$) and sharpens the descriptors that the grounding pins down: the
inter-quartile range of $r_\text{max}$ shrinks to **0.09 vs 0.15 $\text{h}^{-1}$**, of
$E_a$ to 0.26 vs 0.31 eV, and — most strikingly — of $CT_\text{max}$ to **0.22 vs
1.16 °C** (calibrated vs default), because with the melting temperatures held fixed the
upper limit barely moves under correlated envelope sampling. By contrast, the
descriptors that depend directly on the sampled optima are *not* tightened —
$T_\text{opt}$ IQR is 6.0 vs 5.3 °C and niche width 30 vs 21 °C — since the per-enzyme
$T_\text{opt}$ draws ($\rho = 0.7$, sd 4 K) shift the peak (and thus the peak-to-$T_m$
window) more than the hand-set $dT_\text{opt}$ band did. So structured sampling sharpens
the physically pinned descriptors (rate, upper limit, $E_a$) while faithfully
propagating genuine per-enzyme optimum uncertainty into $T_\text{opt}$ and breadth,
which is the intended behaviour for O6.

![**Calibrated thermal-envelope uncertainty.** TPC ensemble under per-enzyme correlated (one-factor $\rho$) thermal sampling — the calibrated-uncertainty fan.](assets/figures/calibrated_ensemble.png){#fig-calens fig-pos="H" width=80%}

![**Nominal vs calibrated descriptor uncertainty.** For each key descriptor, the default hand-set-range distribution (grey) is overlaid with the calibrated per-enzyme correlated distribution (orange).](assets/figures/calibrated_vs_default.png){#fig-calvsdef fig-pos="H" width=85%}

```{python}
#| label: tbl-calibrated
#| tbl-cap: "Calibrated-run descriptor median and interquartile range (per-enzyme correlated envelope sampling, shared fraction ρ = 0.7)."
#| output: asis
import pandas as pd

cal = pd.read_csv("assets/tables/calibrated_descriptors.csv")
keep = [c for c in ["Topt_C", "rmax", "CTmax_C", "niche_width_C", "Ea_eV"] if c in cal.columns]
q = cal[keep].quantile([0.25, 0.5, 0.75])
out = pd.DataFrame({
    "Descriptor": keep,
    "Median": q.loc[0.5].values,
    "IQR": (q.loc[0.75] - q.loc[0.25]).values,
}).round(3)
print(out.to_markdown(index=False))
```

## Proteome-sector allocation trade-off (O3)

Finally, refining the single pool into metabolic, biosynthesis and maintenance sectors
turns the baseline rate into an explicit allocation trade-off with an **interior
optimum**: too little metabolic pool starves enzymes, too little biosynthesis/maintenance
starves translation. @fig-sectrade plots $r_\text{max}$ (and $CT_\text{max}$) against
the metabolic fraction $f_\text{metab}$, coloured by the maintenance fraction
$f_\text{maint}$; @fig-secsens gives the sector-sweep sensitivities.

Across the swept range $f_\text{metab} \in [0.35, 0.65]$, the mean $r_\text{max}$
rises from 0.39 to a **peak of 0.43 $\text{h}^{-1}$ at $f_\text{metab} \approx 0.45$–0.50**
and then falls to 0.27 — a clear interior growth optimum (O3). The sector-sweep
sensitivities are consistent with the whole picture: $r_\text{max}$ is set by
allocation (Spearman $-0.44$ vs $f_\text{metab}$, $-0.47$ vs $f_\text{maint}$: more
maintenance means less rate), while the thermal limits track the optimum shift through
the fixed denaturation ceiling ($CT_\text{max}$ vs $dT_\text{opt}$ $= 0.80$, niche
width vs $dT_\text{opt}$ $= -0.76$) and $E_a$ tracks the curvature (`dCp_scale`
$= 0.52$). The sector model thus supplies the mechanistic allocation axis — in the
maintenance→biosynthesis language of the growth laws — that produces the magnitude
trade-off underpinning O3 and the allocation side of O4.

![**Proteome-sector trade-off.** $r_\text{max}$ and $CT_\text{max}$ versus the metabolic sector fraction $f_\text{metab}$, coloured by maintenance fraction $f_\text{maint}$; the interior $r_\text{max}$ optimum reflects metabolic vs biosynthesis/maintenance co-limitation.](assets/figures/sector_tradeoff.png){#fig-sectrade fig-pos="H" width=90%}

![**Sector-sweep sensitivity.** Spearman indices of TPC descriptors against the sector inputs ($f_\text{metab}$, $f_\text{maint}$, $dT_\text{opt}$, $\Delta C_p$ scale).](assets/figures/sectors_sensitivity.png){#fig-secsens fig-pos="H" width=90%}

# Interpretation and caveats

Taken together, the analyses support a coherent, mechanistic picture that maps onto
the objectives. In the data-grounded unfolding model the thermal **envelope dominates
the whole TPC**: $T_\text{opt}$ tracks the enzyme optima ($\rho = 0.83$); the thermal
limits and breadth are set almost entirely by the per-enzyme melting temperatures
($CT_\text{max}$, $CT_\text{min}$, niche width all $\varphi_E \approx 0.99$); the
rising-limb $E_a$ by the MMRT curvature ($\varphi_E = 0.97$); and even the rate
magnitude is envelope-dominated ($r_\text{max}$ $\varphi_E = 0.78$), with proteome
**allocation** contributing a real but secondary ~20 % to $r_\text{max}$ and
$T_\text{opt}$ and producing the interior sector optimum (O3, O4). Thermal control is
concentrated in a few enzymes (led by argininosuccinate synthase; O2), yet only ~2 % of
per-enzyme parameters are identifiable from growth alone (O5) — the envelope may be
predictable from a small number of sequences, but calibrating the full parameter set
needs omics.

Comparing the two thermal modes makes an important methodological point. Under the
peak-normalised MMRT convention the decomposition showed a clean "allocation sets
magnitude, envelope sets shape" split ($r_\text{max}$ ~100 % allocation); under the
data-grounded unfolding model that split **collapses** and the envelope dominates
$r_\text{max}$ too. This is direct evidence that the tidy separation was **largely a
structural artifact**: peak-normalisation fixes each enzyme's reference $k_\text{cat}$
to its own maximum, so at the optimum enzyme cost is independent of the envelope and
$r_\text{max}$ can only track the pool budget; anchoring $k_\text{cat}$ at $T_\text{opt}$
and fixing the melting temperatures instead lets envelope shifts move the rate. Either
way, the envelope-versus-allocation partition is **under-determined from growth data
alone** [@Pettersen2023]; resolving it requires data that observe the internal state —
temperature-resolved proteomics (for allocation) and $^{13}$C fluxomics (for in-vivo
$k_\text{cat}$) across multiple temperatures. Moreover, real proteome allocation is
itself temperature-dependent: coarse-grained proteome-allocation models of
temperature-dependent growth show reallocation toward chaperone / stress-response
proteins at temperature extremes [@Mairet2021; @Wang2026], so a temperature-dependent
chaperone/stress sector, calibrated to proteomics, would let allocation contribute to
curve *shape*. The decomposition should thus be read as operationalising the
*measurement* of the genome-set (envelope) versus allocation-set (magnitude) split —
the informative quantities being the allocation shares, the interaction terms and the
across-genome variation in the split — rather than as evidence of a fixed biological
law.

Several caveats bound these conclusions, all structural / in-silico:

- **Whole-cell $CT_\text{max}$ vs enzyme $T_m$.** The organism collapses (~47 °C) well
  below the median enzyme melting temperature (~56 °C) because growth needs the *whole*
  proteome folded and functional; the collapse is thus grounded in $T_m$, but the exact
  $CT_\text{max}$ depends on the (few) least-stable rate-limiting enzymes.
- **Transferred thermal parameters.** Per-enzyme $T_m$/$T_\text{opt}$ are joined by
  UniProt id from assembled melting-proteome [@Leuenberger2017] and
  sequence-predictor [@LiEngqvist2019] tables (iML1515-family; 90 % coverage, the rest
  at dataset means), not measured for this exact model; the MMRT (`mmrt`) mode with a
  fitted, breadth-$E_a$-coupled curvature is retained only as a backward-compatible
  baseline (and there gives an unrealistic $CT_\text{max} \approx 76$ °C).
- **Control diagnostic in unfolding mode.** The per-enzyme control coefficients are
  computed on $T_\text{opt}$ (and, in `mmrt` mode, $\Delta C_p$); in unfolding mode the
  falling limb is governed by $T_m$, so a per-enzyme $T_m$ control axis is the natural
  extension (future work), and the $\Delta C_p$ control column is inactive here.
- **Grid-edge descriptors.** $CT_\text{min}$ sits near the grid floor (~9 °C), so it and
  the niche width are lower bounds set partly by the temperature grid.
- **Ranges, not populations.** Variance fractions are defined relative to the swept
  input ranges, not a measured population.
- **First-order identifiability.** The identifiability score is a control-magnitude
  proxy, not a Fisher-information / profile-likelihood analysis.
- **Limited $k_\text{cat}(T)$ data.** DLTKcat has modest skill (log-scale
  $R^2 \approx 0.6$) and currently overlays only 13 model enzymes, so its envelope is
  not yet distinct from the grounded default.
- **Growth only.** Respiration and carbon-use efficiency — the currencies that matter
  for community carbon cycling — are not yet modelled [@Machado2021].

# Next steps

Prioritised, and flagged as already scaffolded in the pipeline (✓) or future work (○):

1. **Ground the falling limb in denaturation ($T_m$), decoupling breadth from $E_a$.**
   ✓ done here: the two-state unfolding model sets $CT_\text{max}$ from per-enzyme
   $T_m$, giving a realistic upper limit independent of the rising-limb $E_a$; the
   `mmrt` scalar-$E_a$ calibration (`calibrate-dcp`) is retained as a fallback. ○ Next:
   add a **per-enzyme $T_m$ control/identifiability axis** (the current control
   diagnostic perturbs $T_\text{opt}$ only) and broaden measured $T_m$ coverage beyond
   90 % of enzymes.
2. **Run the allocation-vs-envelope decomposition on the mechanistic sector axis** —
   done here (✓); extend to report interaction structure across strains.
3. **Constrain the proteome-sector allocation with measured sector fractions** (○).
   Coarse-grained proteome-allocation models of temperature-dependent growth
   [@Mairet2021; @Wang2026] provide *E. coli* sector fractions (metabolic / ribosome /
   chaperone) and their temperature dependence, which can calibrate the sector budgets
   ($P_\text{total}$, $f_\text{metab}$, $f_\text{bio}$) and add a temperature-dependent
   chaperone/stress sector — partially lifting the temperature-independent-allocation
   limitation. Per-enzyme allocation *within* the metabolic sector (hence the full
   envelope/allocation split) still requires temperature-resolved per-protein
   proteomics (the WP1 proteome tier), because coarse sector fractions cannot pin
   ~2500 individual enzymes.
4. **Extend identifiability proteome-wide** — done here (✓, 7680 parameters); next,
   replace the first-order proxy with a profile-likelihood / Fisher analysis (○).
4. **Add respiration and carbon-use-efficiency TPCs** (○), the community-relevant
   currencies, reusing the same generic descriptor machinery.
5. **Broaden the DLTKcat envelope** to cover rate-limiting enzymes so the data-driven
   envelope becomes distinct from the default (○).
6. **Scale the same analyses across the bacterial isolate library** (○) to estimate
   the genome-set-envelope vs allocation-set-magnitude ceiling across taxa — the
   quantity that determines how predictable TPCs are from genomes.
7. **Replace the in-silico priors with phenotype/proteome/flux-calibrated,
   hierarchical-Bayesian posteriors** (○) — the route from structural predictions to
   calibrated, experimentally grounded TPC prediction [@Li2021; @Pettersen2023].
